join_order.label: subquery_semi_join_build
join_order.output: orderkey_sum: hash_map<int32_t, double> (~15M entries); qualifying_set: hash_set<int32_t> of orderkeys where sum > 300 (~few hundred entries)

join_order.label: orders_lookup_via_index
join_order.probe: o_orderkey_hash index (point lookup per qualifying key → row_pos → fetch o_custkey, o_orderdate, o_totalprice)
join_order.note: Avoids full 15M-row orders scan entirely; index lookup is O(1) per qualifying key

join_order.label: customer_lookup_via_index
join_order.probe: o_custkey from each qualifying order → row_pos in customer → fetch c_name varstring
join_order.note: Late materialization: c_name loaded only for qualifying rows

logical_plan.key_reuse: outer SUM(l_quantity) per o_orderkey equals subquery's per-key sum already computed in Phase 1 — no second lineitem scan required

filter_pushdown.comment: Extremely selective: ~few hundred qualifying orderkeys out of ~15M unique orderkeys at SF10

data_structures.subquery_agg: partitioned_hash_map(64 partitions, int32_t key -> double sum); each thread owns one partition determined by key & 63; no contention during parallel scan; single sequential merge pass after
data_structures.qualifying_set: compact open-addressing hash_set<int32_t> (~few hundred entries); built sequentially post-merge by filtering orderkey_sum entries where value > 300.0
data_structures.orders_index: mmap o_orderkey_hash.bin (cap=33554432, HashSlot={int32_t key, uint32_t row_pos}, 8B/slot); hash: h = (uint32_t)(((uint64_t)(uint32_t)key * 0x9E3779B97F4A7C15ULL) >> 39) & (cap-1); linear probe on collision
data_structures.customer_index: mmap c_custkey_hash.bin (cap=4194304, HashSlot={int32_t key, uint32_t row_pos}, 8B/slot); hash: h = (uint32_t)(((uint64_t)(uint32_t)key * 0x9E3779B97F4A7C15ULL) >> 42) & (cap-1); linear probe on collision
data_structures.result_buffer: flat std::vector<ResultRow> (struct of c_name_str, c_custkey, o_orderkey, o_orderdate, o_totalprice, sum_qty); expected size ~few hundred rows
data_structures.topk: std::partial_sort on result_buffer (trivial given tiny cardinality)

phases.phase1_lineitem_scan: parallel (64 threads); each thread processes morsels of 65536 rows; thread t accumulates into partition t = (l_orderkey & 63) for its own bin; actually use thread-local hash_map<int32_t, double> per thread; merge all 64 maps sequentially after parallel phase completes
phases.phase1_qualifying_collection: sequential — single pass over merged orderkey_sum; filter sum > 300; insert to qualifying_set; O(15M) trivial
phases.phase2_index_lookups: sequential — few hundred point lookups into o_orderkey_hash and c_custkey_hash; negligible cost
phases.phase3_final_sort: sequential — std::partial_sort on O(hundreds) result rows for LIMIT 100; trivial

index_usage.purpose: point_lookup_orders: for each qualifying l_orderkey, retrieve row_pos in orders in O(1); then access orders/o_custkey.bin[row_pos], orders/o_orderdate.bin[row_pos], orders/o_totalprice.bin[row_pos]
point_lookup_customer: for each qualifying order's o_custkey, retrieve row_pos in customer in O(1); then access c_name via offsets[row_pos] and data buffer

output_encoding.o_orderdate: int32_t epoch days; reconstruct YYYY-MM-DD using binary search on YEAR_DAYS[] and MONTH_STARTS[leap][month]

breakdown.phase1_lineitem_scan_parallel_64t: ~120ms (720MB streaming sequential read, 64-thread parallel aggregation, warm page cache assumed)
breakdown.phase2_index_lookups_orders_customer: ~2ms (few hundred O(1) point lookups via mmap indexes)
